local_names.romanized_local: Lǜ Dòu (Chinese); Kacang Hijau (Malay); Pachai Payir (Tamil); Sabut Moong (Hindi)

region_of_origin: South Asia (India, Bangladesh); widely cultivated and consumed across Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Legumes — whole cooked mung beans (not split, not sprouted); 1 cup = 202g; GI ~31 (low)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00XX used as closest defensible match for cooked whole mung beans; no more specific FoodEx2 code confirmed in public FoodEx2 catalogue as of April 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for cooked whole mung bean as a food product; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of cooked whole mung beans weighs approximately 202g and provides around 212 kcal, 14.1g protein, 15.4g dietary fibre, and 321mcg folate.

nutrient_source.title: Mung Beans, Mature Seeds, Cooked, Boiled, Without Salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175238); cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) + ICMR-NIN (National Institute of Nutrition, India)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt is a direct match for whole cooked mung beans. Values cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for sabut moong (whole mung). Minor rounding differences resolved in favour of USDA SR Legacy as primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mung Beans, Whole, Cooked — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Research Service)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~31 (low) sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for whole cooked mung beans. Glycemic load per 202g serving calculated as: (31 × 38.8g available carbohydrate per cup) / 100 = 12.0. Available carbohydrate per cup = 202g × 19.2g/100g = 38.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Near-zero sodium (4mg per cup) with high potassium (537mg per cup) and magnesium (97mg per cup)
hypertension.guidance: Whole cooked mung beans are highly suitable for hypertension management: sodium is negligible at 4mg per 202g cup, potassium provides 537mg (approximately 16% of the 3,400mg daily target), and magnesium provides 97mg (approximately 23% RDA), supporting vascular tone and blood pressure regulation — however, traditional preparations such as Chinese 绿豆汤 (sweet mung bean soup) or Malay bubur kacang hijau with coconut milk add significant sugar and saturated fat that should be moderated.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~31) and high dietary fibre (7.6g/100g; 15.4g per cup = 62% RDA)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole cooked mung beans are among the most glycaemically favourable legumes available, with GI ~31 and GL ~12 per cup, making them unrestricted for Type 2 Diabetes management; the exceptionally high folate content (321mcg per cup = 80% RDA) is an additional benefit, but patients should be counselled that traditional sweet preparations (rock sugar in 绿豆汤, coconut milk in bubur kacang hijau) substantially raise the meal glycemic load and should be minimised or avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (7.6g/100g), and high folate (159mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Whole cooked mung beans are cardioprotective: saturated fat is negligible at 0.1g/100g, cholesterol is zero, and the high soluble fibre content supports LDL reduction; the high folate (159mcg/100g; 321mcg per cup) helps manage homocysteine levels, a recognised cardiovascular risk factor, making mung beans a recommended staple for cardiovascular disease prevention across all Singapore communities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highest folate among common legumes (321mcg per cup = 80% RDA); anti-inflammatory flavone glycosides (vitexin, isovitexin) in seed coat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mung beans are particularly valuable for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate therapy, as the high folate content (321mcg per cup) helps offset methotrexate-induced folate depletion; vitexin and isovitexin in the mung bean seed coat have demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in in vitro and animal studies, though clinical evidence in RA patients remains limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whole cooked mung beans (Vigna radiata), also known as green moong dal, sabut moong, kacang hijau (Malay), and 绿豆 (Chinese), are small, oval, olive-green legumes that become soft and tender when boiled. Per 100g cooked: 105 kcal, 7.0g protein, 0.4g fat, 19.2g carbohydrate, 7.6g dietary fibre, 2mg sodium, 266mg potassium, 48mg magnesium, and 159mcg folate — making them one of the most nutrient-dense low-calorie legumes available. Per standard 202g cup: approximately 212 kcal, 14.1g protein, 15.4g fibre (62% RDA), 321mcg folate (80% RDA), and 537mg potassium. Glycemic index is ~31 (low) with a glycemic load of ~12 per cup, placing them among the most glycaemically favourable foods for Type 2 Diabetes management. Mung beans are the most culturally versatile legume in Singapore, consumed across all three major communities: Chinese (绿豆汤 — sweet mung bean soup or tong sui, often with rock sugar and pandan), Indian (sabut moong curry, moong dal preparations), and Malay (bubur kacang hijau — sweet mung bean porridge with coconut milk and palm sugar). The mung beans themselves are clinically unrestricted for T2DM, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease; the sugar and coconut milk in traditional preparations are the primary dietary concerns. Cholesterol is zero, saturated fat is negligible (0.1g/100g), and sodium is near-zero (2mg/100g). The high folate content is clinically significant for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate. Mung beans are naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: is mung bean good for blood pressure
meal_context: Chinese sweet soup (绿豆汤 / tong sui)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 175238) for mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — an exact match. Cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. GI value of 31 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for whole cooked mung beans. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are from approved primary sources with high agreement between USDA and ICMR-NIN.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked mung beans is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175238 or ICMR-NIN tables as individual fatty acid fractions; total fat is low at 0.4g/100g. water_g set to -1: moisture content not extracted from source for this record; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 72.7g water per 100g for cooked mung beans but this field was not populated in the input and is noted here for completeness. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — plant-based legume with no industrial trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food; vitamin B12 is not synthesised by plants). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — mung beans contain negligible vitamin D). GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (31 × 38.8g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.0, where available carbohydrate = 202g × 0.192 = 38.8g. Traditional preparations (绿豆汤 with rock sugar, bubur kacang hijau with coconut milk and palm sugar) significantly alter the nutritional profile; this record covers plain boiled whole mung beans only. FoodEx2 code A00XX used as closest available match; a more specific FoodEx2 code for cooked whole mung beans was not confirmed in the public FoodEx2 catalogue as of April 2026.
